key_insight: Increasing argon flow from 1600 to 1800 L/h during air plasma spraying produced thicker, denser NiCrFeBSiC coatings with better tribological performance.
engineering_relevance: Tuning plasma gas flow is a simple process control lever to improve coating quality, wear resistance, and adhesion in thermal spray applications.
method: Air plasma sprayed self-fluxing NiCrFeBSiC metal-ceramic coatings onto a substrate.
Compared argon flow rates of 1600 and 1800 L/h during coating deposition.
Evaluated structure, microhardness, elastic modulus, friction behavior, and scratch adhesion.
evidence_signal: At 1800 L/h, coating thickness increased about twofold, porosity decreased about twofold, and the friction coefficient dropped to approximately 0.107, with high adhesion indicated by similar critical loads and more gradual failure.
watch_item: The results are based on only two argon flow settings, so the optimal process window and scalability need broader validation.
paper_title: EFFECT OF ARGON FLOW RATE DURING AIR PLASMA SPRAYING ON THE STRUCTURE AND PROPERTIES OF NICRFEBSIC COATINGS
